Title: US court overturns US$16.1bil judgment against Argentina over oil firm seizure
Article:
BUENOS AIRES, Argentina: A US appeals court on Friday overturned a US$16.1 billion judgment against Argentina for nationalising the oil company YPF in 2012.
The ruling was a big victory for President Javier Milei as he tries to boost Argentina's troubled economy.
"We won the YPF trial," Milei wrote in capital letters on the social media platform X, calling the 2-1 ruling by the 2nd US Circuit Court of Appeals in New York the "best possible outcome."
The court struck down a 2023 ruling from Judge Loretta Preska of the US District Court for the Southern District of New York that ordered Argentina to pay US$16.1 billion to minority shareholder companies she said were harmed by the nationalisation of YPF.
The appeals court said Friday that breach of contract claims made by these companies were not recognisable under Argentine law.
The case was heard in the United States rather than Argentina mainly because YPF is listed on the New York Stock Exchange.
Jurisdiction was consistently an issue, as Argentina argued the case should be argued back home but in the end the US courts kept it in New York.
In the expropriation, the Argentine state took over 51 per cent of YPF, which at the time was partially controlled by the Spanish energy giant Repsol.
Two years later Repsol was awarded US$5 billion in damages but smaller shareholders like Petersen Energia Inversora and Eton Park Capital Management, which together controlled a 25.4 per cent stake, got nothing and sued in 2015.
They argued that the government had not made a tender offer, as mandated by Argentine law, to these two companies, which were YPF's second- and third-largest investors.
Argentina has argued that having to pay the settlement – US$18 billion including interest, it says – would cause severe harm to the finances of a country with persistent debt and inflation problems.
It said the settlement would have amounted to a large chunk of its foreign currency reserves.
YPF is an emblematic Argentine company founded in the early 20th century as a state-owned entity but it was privatised in 1993 and eventually came under the control of Repsol.
Then-president Cristina Kirchner re-nationalized YPF in 2012, arguing that it did not produce enough oil and gas to satisfy Argentine demand.
If the US$16.1 billion judgment had been upheld, much of the money would have gone to Britain-based Burford Capital, a company that provides financing for other firms' lawsuits. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Rubio urges world to stop Iran imposing tolls in key strait
Article:
PARIS: US Secretary of State Marco Rubio said Friday he had made progress with allies in opposing Iranian threats to start "tolling" vessels that pass through the strategic Strait of Hormuz.
Iran has partially closed the strait, through which one-fifth of the world's oil normally transits, in retaliation for US-Israeli strikes launched a month ago.
Rubio, after attending talks of the Group of Seven powers, said he had voiced concern that Iran may impose a permanent tolling system "immediately after" the war ends.
"Not only is this illegal, it's unacceptable, it's dangerous to the world, and it's important that the world have a plan to confront it," Rubio told reporters in Paris.
He said he had found "a lot of buy-in" to opposing any Iranian tolling in the strait.
"What we've said is that the countries that are most impacted by that (should) be willing to do something about it, and we'll help them," Rubio said.
"We're willing to be a part of that coalition, but we've encouraged others to sort of put it together," he said.
Rubio said that Britain in particular has "played a prominent role" in bringing together the plan to oppose Iranian tolling.
The Strait of Hormuz was open to international shipping before the US-Israeli attack sparked the war in the Middle East, leading to a surge in global energy prices. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Genting restaurant under probe over RM902 patin dish
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A restaurant in Genting Highlands is under investigation following public uproar over a RM902 steamed patin (silver catfish) dish.
Sinchew reported that the Pahang branch of the Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry conducted checks on Loong Kee Restaurant on Thursday, confirming that the price was clearly stated on the menu at RM33.80 per 100g.
Authorities have issued a notice requiring the restaurant to submit detailed cost and pricing information within four business days to determine whether profiteering is involved.
The controversy arose after a group of 13 tourists were charged RM902 for the dish on March 16.
The fish, weighing 2.7kg, was priced at RM338 per kg.
The restaurant owner maintained that the fish, a wild river patin, is rare and priced based on weight, with no hidden charges.
An employee also said the pricing and type of fish were explained, and a half portion was recommended based on the group size.
However, the owner acknowledged that communication on the total price was insufficient, contributing to the diners' dissatisfaction.
The restaurant has since pledged to improve transparency by informing customers of the total cost of seafood dishes before confirming orders.
It has also reached out to the affected diners to resolve the matter.
Meanwhile, the ministry said businesses must ensure that selling prices do not exceed 100 per cent of the item's cost after factoring in operational expenses, in line with regulations to curb excessive profiteering.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Driver's spatial awareness questioned after 'avoidable' crash
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A dashcam recording of a minor accident has gone viral, showing a red car turning onto a main road and colliding with another vehicle despite ample space to pass.
The video, uploaded on Facebook,
captured the incident
around 10am on March 22.
The exact location was not disclosed.
In the footage, two vehicles can be seen exiting a petrol station and turning right onto the main road.
An oncoming car in the left lane, from which the dashcam footage was filmed, slows down to give them plenty of space.
However, the red car appears to misjudge the clearance and hits the oncoming vehicle while manoeuvring out of the junction.
The impact seemed minor, with no immediate reports of injuries.
The clip has since attracted attention online, with many users commenting on the driver's apparent lack of spatial awareness despite the clear space provided.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: GOF arrests three Thai men for attempting to smuggle petrol, diesel into Thailand
Article:
KANGAR: The General Operations Force (GOF) detained three Thai men who were attempting to smuggle petrol and diesel into Thailand using modified vehicle tanks on Jalan Kangar-Padang Besar, near the Padang Besar Immigration, Customs, Quarantine and Security Complex (ICQS) on Thursday.
Its Northern Brigade Commander, SAC Balveer Singh Mahindar Singh said the police arrested the men, aged between 22 and 45, after inspecting three pickup trucks during 'Op Taring Bravo 1' at 9am.
"As a result of the inspection, it was found that there was a quantity of liquid, suspected to be petrol and diesel, in the modified tanks of each vehicle, believed to be for smuggling petrol and diesel into Thailand," he said in a statement last night.
He said the total amount of petrol and diesel seized amounted to 410 litres, estimated to be worth RM2,015.70, and the three vehicle drivers were believed to be actively involved in smuggling due to the high demand in the neighbouring country, with the total seizure amounting to RM217,015.70 including the vehicles.
"This (smuggling attempt) was due to the lower prices and the reduced stock of petrol and diesel in Thailand as a result of the ongoing global geopolitical crisis," he said, adding that the case is being investigated under Section 21 of the Control of Supplies Act 196, read together with Section 8(4)(a).
He also said that the Perlis Traffic Investigation and Enforcement Department issued summonses to each of the foreign drivers for modifying the vehicles.
He added that the operation was carried out by the police through the GOF Northern Brigade headquarters' Risk Branch, in collaboration with the Perlis branch of the Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry and the Perlis JSPT.
– Bernama
TAG: Perlis, petrol, diesel, seized, tanks, smuggle, GOF, Padang Besar, ICQS, Kangar, Thai men, police, arrested, Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry, traffic, Op Taring, Malaysia, news, NST, region
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: PKR orders two leaders to step down over 'protection money' claims
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: PKR has instructed two leaders linked to a viral video controversy to immediately relinquish their positions in the Federal Territories Residents' Representative Council.
Federal Territories PKR leadership council chairman Datuk Azman Abidin said its political bureau said the directive was issued to R. Vijeyndran and G. Paarthiydasan, who are alleged to have solicited "protection money" from a business premises in Wangsa Maju.
He said the move was necessary to allow police investigations and internal party probes to proceed without interference.
"The Wangsa Maju branch has been instructed to inform both individuals to step down immediately from their roles as the residents' council members," he said in a statement.
The party would not tolerate any form of misconduct, including extortion, protection money or abuse of power, he said.
"Clean governance is a core principle, and the party will not protect any member or representative involved in such practices. Justice must begin within the party itself."
Azman also called on Kuala Lumpur City Hall (DBKL) to step up monitoring of massage parlour activities in the Wangsa Maju area to ensure compliance with regulations, laws and licensing requirements.
The bureau has directed the FT PKR disciplinary committee to conduct a comprehensive internal investigation and report its findings in the near future.
The bureau reminded all party members and representatives that their positions are a public trust that must be upheld with integrity, warning that any misconduct would be dealt with firmly and without compromise.
Police have received three reports in connection with a viral video allegedly involving
extortion
by two men at the massage parlour.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: China is challenging US spaceflight supremacy
Article:
Walking on the Moon by 2030, building a lunar base, and then perhaps on to Mars: after 30 years of honing its expertise, China is challenging the United States' supremacy in spaceflight.
As US space agency Nasa prepares for its crewed lunar flyby mission, Artemis II, let's take a look at China's ambitions in space:
China's crewed space programme —known as Project 921 —was launched on Sept 21, 1992, aiming to develop its own expertise and space station.
The programme has conducted around 15 crewed missions since the first flight of a Chinese astronaut, Yang Liwei, in 2003.
Blocked from the International Space Station (ISS) in 2011 by the US, China built its own orbital hub. The Tiangong, or "Heavenly Palace", space station welcomed its first occupants in 2021.
Three Chinese astronauts —known as "taikonauts"—are currently staying there.
The station allows China to accumulate crucial experience in spacewalks, docking, maintenance and effects on the body.
None of China's crewed launches have been fatal, and the programme is following a long-term roadmap rather than a series of isolated projects.
"This effectiveness stems from strong political commitment at the highest level of the state, stable funding... and the integration of the entire industrial chain into the project," said Richard de Grijs, professor of physics and astronomy at Australia's Macquarie University.
"Compared to the Western, and particularly US, approach, where priorities can shift with each political cycle, this model offers clear advantages in terms of predictability and risk management," he said.
While China's space programme has been historically slower, "it won't change course at the whim of its leadership either".
The Chinese space agency (CNSA) hopes to put astronauts on the Moon by 2030.
China has already sent several robots to the Moon and brought back lunar samples. But a crewed mission requires different equipment, which is currently being tested.
China is set to conduct a test flight of its new Mengzhou ("Dream Ship") spacecraft this year. Replacing the ageing Shenzhou, it will carry astronauts into lunar orbit.
Engineers are also developing a new ultra-powerful rocket around 90m long — the Long March-10, essential for propelling the spacecraft to the Moon. It made its first low-altitude flight on Feb 11.
The Lanyue ("Seize the Moon") lander, which will transport the astronauts from orbit to the lunar surface, could also make its maiden flight between 2028 and 2029.
China hopes to build a basic version of a crewed scientific base, called the International Lunar Research Station, by 2035.
The base will be built near the Moon's south pole, where water in the form of ice is believed to be present.
China is collaborating with Russia on the project.
The base is expected to be built with bricks made on-site from lunar soil using 3D printers. Tested on Earth and on Tiangong, the technique is to be tested on the Moon during the uncrewed Chang'e-8 mission, scheduled for around 2028.
The CNSA says it wants to carry out archaeological research into the origin of the Moon, achieve breakthroughs in strategic technologies and exploit lunar resources.
An expanded version of the base is planned for around 2040.
China is also developing a constellation of relay satellites around the Moon to communicate between its far side and Earth.
China never talks about a "Moon race" or competition with the US.
"They are ambitious for the future of their programme and see the Moon as the logical next step, for its own sake, not for any rivalry.
"I don't think it would change anything for them if America wasn't going," said Jonathan McDowell, astrophysicist and space analyst.
For now, China lags behind the US in terms of crewed flights, according to Chen Lan, a specialist in the Chinese space programme.
Nasa's Dragon and Orion spacecraft remain superior compared to China's Shenzhou, he noted.
"However, I expect when China realises manned lunar landing by 2030 with the new spaceship and lunar landing vehicle, it will catch up with the US in the human spaceflight field," said Chen.
The writer is from AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hot spell sparks four major fires across Pahang, 1,000ha destroyed
Article:
KUANTAN: The ongoing hot spell has sparked four major fire incidents across Pahang, including two at landfill sites, with more than 1,000ha of land already destroyed.
Amid scorching temperatures and prolonged dry conditions, about 70 firefighters, assisted by plantation workers, are working to contain the blaze in three districts - Kuantan, Pekan and Rompin - to prevent the flames from spreading further into surrounding areas.
The fires have affected 735ha of forest and oil palm plantations in Pekan, 300ha of oil palm estates in Kampung Jemeri, Rompin, as well as two separate landfill sites here, where a total of 26ha has been razed.
Pahang Fire and Rescue Department assistant director (operations) Mohd Salahuddin Isa said the fire in Nenasi, Pekan, which has
affected about 735ha of a 2,000-hectare plantation
, entered its sixth day today.
"So far, 338ha have been extinguished, including about 120ha today. Firefighters are working to put out the remaining 396ha.
"Twenty-seven firefighters from eight fire stations, including those from neighbouring districts, along with 10 plantation workers, have been divided into three teams to douse the fire," he said in a statement today.
Meanwhile, Salahuddin said the fire in Kampung Jemeri along Jalan Rompin-Muadzam Shah, now entering its fifth day, has destroyed about 300ha of a 1,214-hectare plantation.
He said 33 firefighters have managed to
douse about 40ha
, with the remaining affected area measuring some 259ha.
Salahuddin said that the landfill fire that broke out at Kampung Bukit Palas in Gebeng here yesterday (March 26) has razed 24ha, of which 9.8ha have been extinguished so far.
He said the fire at the Jerangau-Jabor
landfill in Gebeng
, which started on Tuesday, has affected two hectares and is now under control, with operations involving three firefighters and four workers.
"The landfill firefighting operations at both locations are being carried out using portable pumps with 200-foot-long water hoses," he said.
Salahuddin said firefighting efforts at all four affected areas, which were called off about 7pm today, will resume at 8am tomorrow.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man arrested for slapping teen at LRT station
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police have arrested an unemployed man in connection with an incident in which a teenage boy was slapped while purchasing bread at a shop in the Pasar Seni LRT station here earlier this week.
Dang Wangi police chief Assistant Commissioner Sazalee Adam said the incident occurred at a Rotiboy outlet at about 1.30pm on March 24.
He said the victim was allegedly
slapped in the face
by an unknown man, causing him to fall to the ground and suffer a nosebleed.
"Initial investigations found that the altercation was believed to have stemmed from a misunderstanding while both parties were waiting to purchase bread," he said in a statement.
Following a report, policemen conducted investigations and arrested a 47-year-old local man by the roadside along Jalan Masjid India at about 3.40pm on March 27.
A urine test conducted on the suspect returned negative results, while checks revealed a prior record involving a narcotics-related offence.
Sazalee said the suspect admitted to slapping the victim, claiming he was angered by the victim picking his nose.
Police also seized clothing believed to have been worn during the incident, including a white shirt and a pair of black trousers.
The case is being investigated under Section 323 and Section 506 of the Penal Code, as well as Section 233 of the Communications and Multimedia Act.
"Stern action will be taken against those involved. Police will apply for a remand order on March 28 to facilitate further investigations," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: From ramen to toys, shortages hit Asian manufacturers
Article:
From beer and crisps to noodles, toys and cosmetics, companies —and consumers —across Asia are bracing for a crisis as the Iran war wreaks havoc on supply chains, plastics and oil supplies, upending everyday life and sending prices soaring.
For many, it is already crunch time.
Choi Gun-soo, the manager of a 57-year-old South Korean factory that makes plastic films used by farmers to cover crops as well as by television manufacturers, said his suppliers were raising prices of some raw materials as much as 50 per cent, while other suppliers had simply run out of stock.
"Since we're out of raw materials for some products, we'll have to gradually shut down the machines, and the next one to two weeks is likely to be very critical," he said.
While they had weathered past oil shocks as well as the Covid-19 pandemic, the impact from the war was unprecedented, said Choi, adding that the company had cut production to only 20 per cent to 30 per cent of usual output.
"This is the first time we've been hit this hard. We're really shaken."
At the heart of the supply chain disruption is the Strait of Hormuz, a narrow stretch of water off Iran's southern coast through which roughly one-fifth of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas normally passes.
Asia, which relies more heavily on crude oil, gas, fuel and fertiliser from the Middle East than other parts of the world, is the most vulnerable to supply disruption.
Already prices for some of the fundamentals of modern life such as plastic and rubber are hitting records.
South Korea's Samyang Foods, the maker of the popular spicy Buldak instant ramen noodles, said a prolonged conflict could lead to a shortage of packaging materials and increase costs.
Rival South Korean ramen producer Nongshim said it had two to three months of inventory of packaging material and was preparing for the possibility that the war, which began with United States-Israeli strikes on Iran on Feb 28, could continue.
Yonwoo, a container maker for L'Oreal and K-beauty firms including Amorepacific, said it was scrambling to secure stocks of plastic resin to manufacture the pots used for skincare and cosmetics.
"The issue isn't the price — if supply itself isn't available, then without containers, you simply can't sell the product," said a company official.
The war has triggered fuel shortages around the globe, weakening demand and disrupted supply chains.
In Japan, department store operator Takashimaya said if the crisis persisted there was a possibility that price increases and supply concerns could spread to clothing and household appliances.
Fans of Japan's Wasabeef crisps panicked this month after manufacturer Yamayoshi Seika halted production, blaming a shortage of the heavy oil used for the boilers that heat the oil to fry the snacks.
China's output is set to fall by about a third next month due to the war, according to Xinhua Jing, an analyst at SCI.
Tyremaker Michelin said its supply chain teams were "fully mobilised" and the company was managing and adjusting deliveries to honour its contracts "as much as possible".
In India, the war has already made bottled water more expensive thanks to surging prices of plastic bottles and caps.
Liu Chaonan, whose toy company supplies the likes of US retailer Walmart, said soaring raw materials costs were taking a toll.
"The situation in Iran is having a very significant impact on our toy industry," said Liu, who employs more than 150 people.
"We will likely make price adjustments when quoting new products."
Higher crude prices feed directly into retail fuel prices, raising the cost of petrol, diesel, aviation fuel, cooking gas, and business and manufacturing operations worldwide.
Dominic Desmarais, chief solutions officer at Liya Solutions, which connects companies with suppliers in China making everything from furniture to titanium products, said the price of anything made from petroleum was rising.
The writers are from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Report: Eight Israelis detained at KLIA
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: At least eight Israeli citizens were detained at Kuala Lumpur International Airport (KLIA) in recent months, according to a report by Israeli news outlet Ynet.
The report claimed that some of those detained held dual nationality, while others were travelling solely on Israeli passports.
It said the individuals were either visiting Malaysia or transiting through KLIA en route to other Asian destinations, amid the absence of formal diplomatic ties between Malaysia and Israel.
According to the report, four young Israelis were detained on Wednesday while in transit to the Philippines.
Among them were two women who spoke only Hebrew and were travelling on Israeli passports.
The travellers reportedly said they had consulted a chatbot on the safety of transiting through Kuala Lumpur from Thailand to the Philippines and were advised that it was safe. However, they later opted to return to Thailand.
In a separate incident, two Israeli men travelling from Cambodia to the Philippines via Malaysia were detained for two days before being deported back to Cambodia.
The New Straits Times has reached out to the Malaysian Border Control and Protection Agency (MCBA) and is awaiting a response.
In each case, Israel's embassy in Singapore, led by Ambassador Eliyahu Vered Hazan and Consul Noa Oved, intervened to secure the release of the detainees, either allowing them to continue their journey or returning them to their point of origin.
Hazan warned Israelis against travelling to Malaysia.
"When lives are at stake, you cannot say 'it will be fine,'" he was quoted as saying.
"I want to emphasise the National Security Council's travel warning regarding Malaysia. The recommendation is clear, and I join it.
"Avoid travelling to Malaysia and do not transit there. All those detained had not broken any law or committed any offence."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: NST Leader: How to be prudent during times of crisis
Article:
Recent fluctuations in food and fuel prices are not just isolated economic inconveniences, but clear signals for the public to adopt a more sophisticated understanding of supply chains and personal resource management.
The dinner table and the petrol pump are the most sensitive barometers of national stability.
However, the current spikes in vegetable prices and fuel choices should not be seen as a harbinger of collapse, but as a call for a more mindful, resilient approach to how we consume.
Take the projected rise in vegetable prices caused by the nexus of local drought conditions and geopolitical instability (Middle East conflict).
Farmers' associations are already warning that prices may surge by 50 per cent before settling at 30 per cent. Malaysia is heavily dependent on imported fertilisers.
Between four to five million tonnes of fertilisers valued at more than RM5 billion are imported annually, mainly from Russia, China, Canada, the United States and Vietnam.
About one-third of the country's imports pass through the Strait of Hormuz, the crucial sea passage for goods that Iran has closed, making Malaysia vulnerable to shipping reroutes that could add close to two weeks to transit times.
Domestic production only meets about 39 per cent of local farmers' needs.
The war in the Middle East is also shaping a new trend among Malaysians with wallet anxiety. There has been a 50 per cent drop in RON97 sales as motorists migrate to the subsidised RON95, signalling a sharp tightening of household budgets.
One reason for the shift is said to be the price of RON97 being increased to RM5.15 per litre for the week of March 26 to April 1.
How then can Malaysian consumers adopt a more resilient and mindful approach to consumption? First of all, they need to understand the link between the drought in the north and the price of greens in the city.
Similarly, there is a link between the far-away Strait of Hormuz and the prices of fuels, fertilisers and food, especially when one-third of our goods come through the choke point.
Having understood this, we can make rational substitutions rather than emotional complaints.
Prudence is an all-time solution, but more so during circumstances such as the country is facing now.
And prudence means switching to alternative produce during seasonal droughts rather than demanding the same goods at any price.
There will be those who give in to reactive hoarding. The sooner they realise that it causes artificial hikes in prices, the better.
Be like the rational users of fuel who prudently shifted to RON95 when RON97's price increased, we tell those who hoard. Prudent adjustment to economic reality pays.
With more than 75 per cent of Malaysians living in cities, farming, too, needs prudent adjustment.
One way is to make urban farming a sustainable reality in the cities. Urban farms reduce the prices of produce by reducing the distance between the farm and fork, but also the 30 per cent of food lost in transportation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police rescue 14, arrest 7 in Melaka human trafficking raids
Article:
MELAKA: Police detained seven people for alleged involvement in human trafficking and rescued 14 others believed to be victims of forced labour here today.
Melaka Police chief Datuk Dzulkhairi Mukhtar said two raids were carried out by a team from the D3 Anti-Trafficking in Persons and Anti-Smuggling of Migrants (Atipsom) Division, Criminal Investigation Department (CID) from Bukit Aman, at Taman Klebang Besar and Jalan Tengkera here at about 1am.
"In the operation, seven suspects aged between 19 and 38 were detained, comprising a man from China, a Taiwanese woman, a Myanmar man, and four women from Myanmar, the Philippines and Indonesia.
"All suspects were arrested under Section 12 of the Atipsom Act 2007 for allegedly being involved in forced labour exploitation activities, and are also being investigated under Section 6(1)(c) of the Immigration Act 1959/63," he said in a statement today.
Dzulkhairi said that in the same operation, police also rescued 14 victims aged between 25 and 36, comprising seven men and six women from the Philippines, as well as one woman from Myanmar.
He said initial investigations found that all the victims were believed to have been trafficked for forced labour exploitation in online scam activities.
"The victims were initially promised jobs as customer service personnel, but upon arriving in the country, they were forced to be involved in the scam activities.
"They were also believed to have been subjected to debt bondage, with their passports confiscated by the management, placed under strict control and not allowed to leave, and were even forced to work overtime against their will," he said.
All those arrested, as well as the victims, had been handed over to the investigating officer at the Melaka Tengah district police headquarters for further action, he said.
"An application for an Interim Protection Order (IPO) for the 14 victims has been made for 21 days starting today until April 16, 2026," he said.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Container falls off speeding trailer, causes traffic snarl in Port Klang [WATCH]
Article:
SHAH ALAM: Traffic was congested along Jalan Kem in Port Klang, near here, today after a container fell off a trailer believed to have been speeding.
South Klang district police chief ACP Lim Jit Huey said the incident, which occurred at about noon, did not result in any casualties or damage to other vehicles.
"Initial investigations found that the trailer was believed to be speeding when passing through the location, causing the container to become detached from the trailer before falling onto the road.
"Other vehicles using the route managed to stop in time, avoiding a collision. Checks on the 30-year-old male driver found that he tested negative for alcohol and drugs," he said in a statement today.
In the same statement, Lim warned drivers of commercial vehicles not to drive recklessly or they will face stern action, as such behaviour could endanger other road users.
The case is being investigated under Section 43(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987.
Earlier, a video of the incident went viral on social media. – BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar concludes Jakarta visit after meeting with Prabowo
Article:
JAKARTA: Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim concluded his special visit to Jakarta today after meeting Indonesian President Prabowo Subianto at the Istana Merdeka here.
The meeting, which commenced at 4.30pm local time, took place against the backdrop of escalating geopolitical developments in Middle East.
Following the discussions, Prabowo accompanied Anwar to Halim Perdanakusuma Airport as a gesture of goodwill and cordial ties between the two leaders.
He departed for home at about 7.15pm local time, concluding his one-day
working visit
.
The visit was undertaken at Prabowo's invitation following a phone conversation between the two leaders on March 23, and marked Anwar's first trip to Jakarta this year.
The two leaders had held five high-level exchanges last year, including the 13th Malaysia-Indonesia Annual Consultation on July 28 in Jakarta.
Anwar, in a statement issued after today's meeting, said Malaysia and Indonesia agreed to intensify diplomatic efforts to de-escalate the conflict in Middle East, protect civilian lives, and open space for negotiations towards a peaceful and lasting resolution.
Amid growing global uncertainty, both countries also stressed the need to strengthen principled regional cohesion to preserve peace, ensure stability, and enhance economic resilience for the well-being of the people, he said.
During the approximately two-hour meeting, both leaders further emphasised the importance of ensuring the continuity of global supply chains and strategic trade routes, including the Strait of Hormuz, remain unaffected.
"Amid current global uncertainties, the cooperation between Malaysia and Indonesia has become increasingly important, with both countries agreeing to continue working closely together, strengthen strategic cooperation, and intensify diplomatic efforts to ensure regional stability and the well-being of their citizens.
"May the ties between these two close nations continue to be strengthened to an even more solid and respected level, Insya-Allah," Anwar said in a Facebook post.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Philippines shifts preparatory meetings for 2026 Asean chairmanship to virtual formats
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Philippines has shifted preparatory meetings for its 2026 Asean Chairmanship to virtual formats, citing developments in Middle East and their impact on regional priorities, particularly energy security.
Executive Secretary Ralph G. Recto said President Ferdinand R. Marcos Jr. has ordered a recalibration of the Philippines' hosting of Asean 2026, scaling down non-essential activities "in order to save on expenses and focus on the most important".
As part of this directive, approximately 650 preparatory meetings will now be conducted online, a press statement posted on the Chair's official website said Friday.
It said the upcoming Asean Summit in May will proceed as an in-person meeting but on a "very bare bones" format.
The Summit is expected to tackle urgent priorities, including energy security, food security, and migrant workers' concerns.
According to the statement, all host agencies have been directed to conduct all preparatory meetings, from working group to ministerial, virtually.
It affirmed that despite the changes in format of meetings, Asean cooperation remains steady and purposeful, ensuring that the region continues to respond collectively and decisively to shared challenges.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Youth groups, parties slam Philippine senator over call to 'revive' Sabah claim amid fuel crisis
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Political parties and youth groups have slammed a Philippine senator for calling to revive Manila's claim over Sabah, calling it "irresponsible and provocative."
PKR Youth (AMK) communications director Nurhanani Dhamirah said such remarks risk straining bilateral ties between the two countries.
She said the attempt to link Sabah's sovereignty with its petroleum resources was particularly concerning, suggesting that such narratives reflect efforts to politicise territorial issues for narrow economic interests amid the ongoing global fuel crisis.
"Such remarks carry the potential to trigger geopolitical tensions in the Southeast Asian region," she said in a statement today.
This comes after Senator Robin Padilla urged President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. to revive the Philippines' longstanding claim over Sabah, citing the territory's rich oil reserves amid the ongoing global fuel crisis.
Padilla argued that Sabah, located on the island of Borneo, remains historically tied to the Philippines and suggested that its petroleum resources could help address the country's energy concerns.
The call comes as fuel prices continue to be a pressing issue globally, heightening interest in resource-rich territories
Nurhanani said that Sabah is a legitimate and inseparable part of Malaysia, and any move to revive what she described as outdated and unfounded claims contravenes international law and the principles of regional harmony.
She added that arguments based on the historical claims of the Sulu Sultanate have no legal standing under modern sovereignty, particularly following Sabah's lawful exercise of self-determination.
Nurhanani urged the government to summon the Philippine diplomatic representative to seek an official explanation over the statement, while also strengthening Malaysia's historical and legal narrative on Sabah at the global level.
She also called for enhanced security preparedness in Sabah's waters and border areas as a precautionary measure.
Meanwhile, Parti Gagasan Rakyat Sabah's youth wing has dismissed attempts to link Sabah to external economic issues, stressing that the state is not a commodity to resolve challenges faced by other countries.
Its acting chief, Azrul Ibrahim, said Sabah remains an integral part of a sovereign nation, adding that no party can question its position within the Malaysian federation.
He also criticised efforts to tie Sabah's status to ongoing oil and gas concerns, describing such arguments as outdated and unwarranted.
Azrul said these claims appeared to be an attempt to divert attention from internal crises faced by certain parties.
Angkatan Belia Islam Malaysia (Abim) also called on the Philippine government, as the current Asean chair, to distance itself from any statements that contradict the bloc's principles, in order to preserve mutual respect, justice and regional peace.
It strongly condemned the statement by a Philippine senator calling for the revival of the Sabah claim, describing it as baseless and provocative.
The organisation said the claim, which was linked to Sabah's oil wealth, lacked historical and legal grounding, and warned that such rhetoric could undermine regional stability.
Abim called on the government to adopt a firmer and clearer stance in defending national sovereignty through principled diplomatic channels.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MOH relaunches HSOP to speed up MRI services at HKL
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Health Ministry (MOH) has reintroduced the Hospital Services Outsourcing Programme (HSOP) at Kuala Lumpur Hospital (HKL) to ease congestion and reduce waiting times for patients requiring magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) services.
In a statement today, HKL said the HSOP reduced patient waiting times by 33 per cent in 2025, from 24 weeks to 16 weeks.
"In 2025, HKL optimised 96.8 per cent of its allocated slots, using 365 out of 377.
"For this year, the hospital has applied for 640 slots, a 70 per cent increase, to shorten patient waiting times even more," read the statement.
The programme was officially relaunched on Wednesday after the approval of new funding on March 19.
HKL said its Radiology Department operates two MRI machines in the Main Block, with capacity for 24 to 28 cases a day, depending on case complexity and patient condition.
"However, daily scans have sometimes reached 36 to prioritise emergency and critical cases that need urgent attention.
"HKL and the MOH remain committed to providing efficient healthcare services that meet the highest standards of care," the statement said.
Previously, a portal reported that HKL's limited MRI services had forced the hospital to refer patients to private facilities for scans.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fisherman killed in crash hours before engagement
Article:
KUALA TERENGGANU: Twenty-five-year-old Ain Sofea Aminu Nansar had hoped to be engaged to her partner, 33-year-old Mohamad Shahrin Mohd, by the end of today.
It never happened.
What was meant to be a joyous day for the couple turned into tragedy when Shahrin, a fisherman, was killed after the motorcycle he was riding crashed into a road barrier this morning.
Ain said she never imagined that their final meeting last night would become their last memory before receiving the devastating news.
She said they were supposed to have their engagement ceremony at 3pm today at her family home in Kampung Manir, near here.
"God loved him more. Last night, I met him and gave him the outfit he was supposed to wear for our engagement today. But I never imagined he would not live to wear it," she said.
"I accept this fate, although I am saddened that we did not get to be engaged," she added.
Meanwhile, the victim's brother, Mohamad Hafiz Mohd, 36, said his younger sibling had shown changes in behaviour prior to the incident.
He said that on the first day of Syawal, Shahrin had insisted on taking photos with all his nieces and nephews.
"Previously, he was known to be reluctant to take photos and would only do so when persuaded.
"But that day, he was the one eagerly asking all his nieces and nephews to take photos together.
"Perhaps it was a sign that he would leave us forever," he said.
Shahrin was laid to rest at 5pm at the Cengal Muslim Cemetery in Pulau Duyong.
Kuala Terengganu police chief Assistant Commissioner Azli Mohd Noor said police were alerted to the accident along Jalan Kampung Duyong at around 7.30am.
He said the victim had been travelling from Kuala Terengganu towards his home in Kampung Duyong Kecil.
"We believe he lost control before crashing into a road barrier on the left side of the road and died at the scene.
"The case is being investigated under Section 41(1) of the Road Transport Act," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd
